title: Feathers, Fists, and Festivals: The Winners of Smithsonian's Photo Contest
summary:
Imagine three tiny birds with feathers sticking up like punk rockers' mohawks, squabbling and pecking at each other. That's the scene that won the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest. The photo, titled "The Punk Rockers," was taken by Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian from Perkiomenville, Pennsylvania. She captured the egret chicks (a type of white bird) at a rookery (a breeding ground) under a ramp at the Ocean City welcome center in New Jersey. "They were squabbling nonstop," she said. The chicks were no larger than golf balls when curled up, and their parent loomed in the background with long legs and imposing feet.

Over 17,000 submissions from 108 countries were entered. The other six winners are equally captivating. One shows a barefoot devotee expressing herself during the Hindu festival of Shivaratri, where devotees light oil lamps in prayer. Another captures a 14-year-old Navajo girl, DelShay Henio, during her Kinaaldá ceremony (a traditional coming-of-age ritual), leading her horse Hazel into the wind. A conservation photographer in Johannesburg captured a rare, colorful chameleon in a garden. In Sicily, a retired photographer documented men carrying heavy floats (weighing hundreds of pounds) during an intense Holy Week procession. An aerial photo shows the circular Oppo Chang An R&D Center in China, with an 820-foot office tower. Finally, a travel photographer captured a Mundari child in South Sudan cleaning a bull's horns with mud and water.

Burke, who switched from bird-watching to photography in 2014, said, "We need to save species like this and save moments like this." The contest invites us to take a closer look at the world's beauty and diversity.
why_it_matters: The contest showcases how photography can capture both natural beauty and cultural traditions, reminding us to appreciate and protect the world's diversity. It also shows that amateur photographers can make a difference by documenting what they see.

keywords.explanation: The title of the winning photo, referring to the birds' feather mohawks.
Young white birds that were squabbling in the photo.
The top award in the Smithsonian Magazine contest.
An animal doctor; the photographer Jacqueline Burke is a veterinarian.
A breeding ground for birds, where the photo was taken.
The number of photos entered in the contest from different countries.

background_read: Smithsonian Magazine's annual photo contest attracts thousands of entries from around the world, showcasing diverse subjects from nature to culture.
Egrets are long-legged wading birds in the heron family, often found in wetlands and coastal areas.
The other winning photos capture ceremonies and traditions from different cultures, such as the Navajo Kinaaldá and the Hindu Shivaratri festival.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Three tiny birds with mohawk-like feathers squabbling.
└─ specific: 'the photo titled "The Punk Rockers" won grand prize.'
KEY EVENT: Jacqueline Burke captures the scene at Ocean City welcome center rookery.
├─ WHERE: Under a ramp at Ocean City welcome center in New Jersey.
└─ DETAILS: Chicks were no larger than golf balls, parent loomed in background.
├─ Spirituality: barefoot devotee at Shivaratri festival.
├─ Tradition: Navajo girl DelShay Henio during Kinaaldá ceremony.
├─ Culture: men carrying heavy floats in Sicilian Holy Week procession.
├─ Architecture: aerial view of Oppo Chang An R&D Center in China.
└─ Resilience: Mundari child cleaning bull's horns in South Sudan.
CLOSING: Burke's message: 'We need to save species like this and save moments like this.'

perspectives.description: The contest recognizes the skill and creativity of photographers worldwide, giving them a platform to share unique perspectives.
Some critics might argue that the contest focuses on aesthetically pleasing or exotic images, potentially overlooking more serious documentary photography or underrepresenting certain regions.
The wide range of subjects—from nature to cultural ceremonies—reflects the global reach of the contest and the increasing accessibility of photography as a hobby.
As photography becomes more common with smartphones, contests like this may evolve to include new formats and themes, encouraging young people to participate.
